section.title: Overview
section.para: The Entity Linker API lets an application associate named entities with records in an external data source. For example, a location found by a Name Finder can be matched to a gazetteer entry, or a person can be matched to a directory record. OpenNLP provides the linking interfaces and data types; applications supply the implementation and data source.

An EntityLinker receives the document text, sentence and token spans, and the name spans produced for each sentence. It returns application-defined spans that hold the links. LinkedSpan and BaseLink are support classes for this result; they are not linker implementations.

section.title: Preparing Input Spans
section.para: The four arguments passed to find use two coordinate systems. Sentence and token spans are character offsets in the complete document. Name spans are token indexes within their sentence, as returned by NameFinderME. The outer indexes of tokensBySentence and namesBySentence must correspond to the same entry in sentences.

section.title: Implementing an Entity Linker
section.para: Implement EntityLinker<T>, where T extends Span. Load reusable resources in init. Implement the document overload of find by visiting each sentence, and implement the sentence overload with the actual lookup logic.

A production implementation should validate array lengths and name boundaries before indexing into tokens, normalize lookup keys consistently, and define how multiple candidate records are scored and ordered.

section.title: Loading an Implementation
section.para:
EntityLinkerFactory loads an implementation through OpenNLP's extension loader and then calls its init method. With the overload that accepts an entity type, the key is linker.<entityType>:
linker.location=com.example.GazetteerLinker gazetteer.path=/data/locations.tsv

section.title: Command Line Tool
section.para: The EntityLinker command loads the linker.location implementation from a properties file. It reads blank-line-separated documents in the native Name Finder sample format from standard input and writes each linked span to standard output.

Each non-empty input line is a tokenized sentence whose names use <START:type> and <END> markers. For example:
The office is in <START:location> New York <END> .
The second document starts after the blank line .
